source_refs.html: /Users/toy/English/躺着学/躺着学阅读/8182_Passage 1 What are you laughing at.html

passages.title: What are you laughing at?
passages.source_html:
What are you laughing at? 


A 


We like to think that laughing is the height of human sophistication. Our big brains let us see the humour in a strategically positioned pun, an unexpected plot twist or a clever piece of wordplay. But while joking and wit are uniquely human inventions, laughter certainly is not. Other creatures, including chimpanzees, gorillas and even rats, chuckle. Obviously, they don’t crack up at Homer Simpson or titter at the boss’s dreadful jokes, but the fact that they laugh in the first place suggests that sniggers and chortles have been around for a lot longer than we have. It points the way to the origins of laughter, suggesting a much more practical purpose than you might think. 


B 


There is no doubt that laughing typical involves groups of people. ‘Laughter evolved as a signal to others – it almost disappears when we are alone,’ says Robert Provine, a neuroscientist at the University of Maryland. Provine found that most laughter comes as a polite reaction to everyday remarks such as ‘see you later’, rather than anything particularly funny. And the way we laugh depends on the company we’re keeping. Men tend to laugh longer and harder when they are with other men, perhaps as a way of bonding. Women tend to laugh more and at a higher pitch when men are present, possibly indicating flirtation or even submission.


C 


To find the origins of laughter, Provine believes we need to look at the play. He points out that the masters of laughing are children, and nowhere is their talent more obvious than in the boisterous antics, and the original context plays,’ he says. Well-known primate watchers, including Dian Fossey and Jane Goodall, have long argued that chimps laugh while at play. The sound they produce is known as a panting laugh. It seems obvious when you watch their behavior – they even have the same ticklish spots as we do. But remove the context, and the parallel between human laughter and a chimp’s characteristic pant laugh is not so clear. When Provine played a tape of the pant laughs to 119 of his students, for example, only two guessed correctly what it was.


D 


These findings underline how chimp and human laughter vary. When we laugh the sound is usually produced by chopping up a single exhalation into a series of shorter with one sound produced on each inward and outward breath. The question is: does this pant laughter have the same source as our own laughter? New research lends weight to the idea that it does. The findings come from Elke Zimmerman, head of the Institute for Zoology in Germany, who compared the sounds made by babies and chimpanzees in response to tickling during the first year of their life. Using sound spectrographs to reveal the pitch and intensity of vocalizations, she discovered that chimp and human baby laughter follow broadly the same pattern. Zimmerman believes the closeness of baby laughter to chimp laughter supports the idea that laughter was around long before humans arrived on the scene. What started simply as a modification of breathing associated with enjoyable and playful interactions has acquired a symbolic meaning as an indicator of pleasure. 


E 


Pinpointing when laughter developed is another matter. Humans and chimps share a common ancestor that lived perhaps 8 million years ago, but animals might have been laughing long before that. More distantly related primates, including gorillas, laugh, and anecdotal evidence suggests that other social mammals may do too. Scientists are currently testing such stories with a comparative analysis of just how common laughter is among animals. So far, though, the most compelling evidence for laughter beyond primates comes from research done by Jaak Panksepp from Bowling Green State University, Ohio, into the ultrasonic chirps produced by rats during play and in response to tickling.


F 


All this still doesn’t answer the question of why we laugh at all. One idea is that laughter and tickling originated as a way of sealing the relationship between mother and child. Another is that the reflex response to tickling is protective, alerting us to the presence of crawling creatures that might harm us or compelling us to defend the parts of our bodies that are most vulnerable in hand-to-hand combat. But the idea that has gained most popularity in recent years is that laughter in response to tickling is a way for two individuals to signal and test their trust in one another. This hypothesis starts from the observation that although a little tickle can be enjoyable if it goes on too long it can be torture. By engaging in a bout of tickling, we put ourselves at the mercy of another individual, and laughing is a signal that we laughter is what makes it a reliable signal of trust according to Tom Flamson, a laughter researcher at the University of California, Los Angels. ‘Even in rats, laughter, tickle, play and trust are linked. Rats chirp a lot when they play,’ says Flamson, ‘These chirps can be aroused by tickling. And they get bonded to us as a result, which certainly seems like a show of trust.’ 


G 

We’ll never know which animal laughed the first laugh, or why. But we can be sure it wasn’t in response to a prehistoric joke. The funny thing is that while the origins of laughter are probably quite serious, we owe human laughter and our language-based humor to the same unique skill. While other animals pant, we alone can control our breath well enough to produce the sound of laughter. Without that control, there would also be no speech – and no jokes to endure.

groups.title: Questions 1-13
groups.source_html:
Questions 1-6


Look at the following research findings (questions 1-6) and the list of people below.  Match each finding with the correct person A, B, C or D.  Write the correct letter, A, B, C or D, in boxes 1-6 on your answer sheet. 


NB you may use any letter more than once.


A Tom Flamson 

B Elke Zimmerman 

C Robert Provine 

D Jaak Panksepp 


Question / ParagraphABCDEFG
1

1 Babies and chimps produce similar sounds of laughter. 
✅ 正确答案：B

原文答案句：The findings come from Elke Zimmerman, head of the Institute for Zoology in Germany, who compared the sounds made by babies and chimpanzees in response to tickling during the first year of their life. Using sound spectrographs to reveal the pitch and intensity of vocalizations, she discovered that chimp and human baby laughter follow broadly the same pattern.  

对应段落：D段  

审题：题目问的是哪个研究者发现婴儿和黑猩猩发出相似的笑声。  

定位关键信息：题目中的关键词是“Babies and chimps produce similar sounds of laughter”，通过这些词可以定位到D段，该段明确提到Elke Zimmerman的研究发现婴儿和黑猩猩的笑声模式相似。  

解题思路：原文中D段提到Elke Zimmerman通过对比婴儿和黑猩猩在被挠痒时的声音，发现它们的笑声模式基本相同，这与题目描述完全一致。因此，答案是B。
2

2 Primates are not the only animals who produce laughter.
✅ 正确答案：D

原文答案句：More distantly related primates, including gorillas, laugh, and anecdotal evidence suggests that other social mammals may do too. Scientists are currently testing such stories with a comparative analysis of just how common laughter is among animals. So far, though, the most compelling evidence for laughter beyond primates comes from research done by Jaak Panksepp from Bowling Green State University

对应段落：E段

审题：题目问的是哪个研究者发现“灵长类动物不是唯一会笑的动物”。

定位关键信息：利用题干中的关键词“Primates are not the only animals who produce laughter”定位到文章中关于动物笑的普遍性的信息。

解题思路：原文E段提到“More distantly related primates, including gorillas, laugh, and anecdotal evidence suggests that other social mammals may do too.”这句话明确指出，不仅灵长类动物如大猩猩会发笑，其他社会性哺乳动物也可能发笑。这与题干“灵长类动物不是唯一会发笑的动物”吻合，后文又提到Panksepp为这一观点提供了有力证据，因此正确答案是D。
3

3 Laughter also suggests that we feel safe and easy with others.
✅ 正确答案：A

原文答案句：By engaging in a bout of tickling, we put ourselves at the mercy of another individual, and laughing is a signal that we laughter is what makes it a reliable signal of trust according to Tom Flamson, a laughter researcher at the University of California, Los Angels.

对应段落：F段  

审题：题目问的是哪个研究者发现“笑也表明我们感到安全且与他人相处融洽”。  

定位关键信息：利用题干中的关键词"feel safe and easy with others"（感到安全且与他人相处融洽）来定位原文中的相关表述。  

解题思路：原文F段提到近年来最流行的观点是，对搔痒的反应性笑是两个人用来相互传递并测试彼此信任的一种方式，Flamson又提到，让别人挠痒痒时，我们将自己置于他人的控制之下，这时的笑是一种信任的表现，这与题干中“笑声表明我们感到安全且与他人相处融洽”的意思一致。因此，该研究发现对应的是A选项。
4

4 Laughter is a response to a polite situation instead of humour.
✅ 正确答案：C

原文答案句：Provine found that most laughter comes as a polite reaction to everyday remarks such as ‘see you later’, rather than anything particularly funny.  

对应段落：B段  

审题：题目问的是哪个研究者发现“笑声是对礼貌情境的反应，而不是幽默”。  

定位关键信息：根据题目中的关键词“polite situation”和“instead of humour”定位到B段中“most laughter comes as a polite reaction to everyday remarks such as ‘see you later’, rather than anything particularly funny.”这句话。  

解题思路：原文中明确提到罗伯特·普罗文（Robert Provine）的研究发现，大部分笑声是对日常礼貌性话语的反应，而不是对幽默的反应。这与题目中的描述完全一致，因此答案为C。
5

5 Animal laughter evolved before human laughter.
✅ 正确答案：B

原文答案句：Zimmerman believes the closeness of baby laughter to chimp laughter supports the idea that laughter was around long before humans arrived on the scene.

对应段落：D段

审题：题目问的是哪个研究者发现动物笑声是否在人类笑声之前进化。

定位关键信息：通过研究发现的核心理念 “Animal laughter evolved before human laughter” 进行定位，寻找文中表述此观点的研究者。

解题思路：题目中的研究发现是“动物笑声的进化早于人类笑声”。在段落D中，研究者Elke Zimmerman通过比较婴儿和黑猩猩的笑声，提出了她的观点：婴儿笑声与黑猩猩笑声的相似性，支持了“笑声在人类出现之前早已存在(long before humans arrived on the scene)”这一理念。这与研究发现“动物笑声的进化早于人类笑声”完全吻合。因此，提出此研究发现的研究者是Elke Zimmerman，对应选项B。
6

6 Laughter is a social activity.
✅ 正确答案：C

原文答案句：‘Laughter evolved as a signal to others – it almost disappears when we are alone,’ says Robert Provine, a neuroscientist at the University of Maryland. Provine found that most laughter comes as a polite reaction to everyday remarks such as ‘see you later’, rather than anything particularly funny. And the way we laugh depends on the company we’re keeping.

对应段落：B段  

审题：题目问的是哪个研究者发现笑是一种社交活动。  

定位关键信息：通过研究发现的核心概念 “Laughter is a social activity” 进行定位，寻找文中明确将 laughter 与 social, groups, others 等概念联系起来的研究者。

解题思路：题目中的研究发现是“笑是一种社交活动”。在段落B中，研究者Robert Provine明确指出“笑是作为给他人的信号进化而来的——当我们独处时，笑声几乎消失”，并且进一步说明“我们笑的方式取决于我们和谁在一起”。这些陈述直接、有力地证明了笑的社交属性，与研究发现完全一致。因此，提出此研究发现的研究者是Robert Provine，对应选项C。

Questions 7-10


Complete the summary using the list of words, A-K, below. Choose the correct word and move it into the gap.


Options (Word Bank):
A. evolution
B. chirps
C. origins
D. voice
E. confidence
F. rats
G. primates
H. response
I. play
J. children
K. tickling
7

Some researchers believe that laughter first evolved out of 7  .


正确答案： I

原文答案句：To find the origins of laughter, Provine believes we need to look at the play.

对应段落：C段

审题：题目问的是研究人员认为笑声最初是从什么发展而来的。

定位关键信息：利用题目中的关键词"laugh first evolved out of"定位到C段中提到笑声起源的句子。

解题思路：原文明确指出笑声的起源与"play"（玩耍）有关，即笑声最初是从玩耍中发展出来的。题目中的"laugh first evolved"对应原文中的"origins of laughter"，而"play"是笑声起源的关键因素。因此，正确答案是I。


8

The investigation has revealed that human and chimp laughter may have the same 8  .

正确答案： C

原文答案句：Using sound spectrographs to reveal the pitch and intensity of vocalizations, she discovered that chimp and human baby laughter follow broadly the same pattern. Zimmerman believes the closeness of baby laughter to chimp laughter supports the idea that laughter was around long before humans arrived on the scene. What started simply as a modification of breathing associated with enjoyable and playful interactions has acquired a symbolic meaning as an indicator of pleasure.

对应段落：D段

审题：题目问的是研究发现人类和黑猩猩的笑声可能有相同的什么。

定位关键信息：通过题目中的关键词“human and chimp laughter”和“same”可以定位到D段中关于Elke Zimmerman的研究，她比较了人类婴儿和黑猩猩在搔痒时的声音。

解题思路：根据原文中的句子“Zimmerman believes the closeness of baby laughter to chimp laughter supports the idea that laughter was around long before humans arrived on the scene. ”可以得出，人类和黑猩猩的笑声可能有相同的起源或基础，即“modification of breathing”。因此，正确答案是“C”。


9

Besides, scientists have been aware that 9  laugh, however, it now seems that laughter might be more widespread than once we thought.

正确答案： G

原文答案句： More distantly related primates, including gorillas, laugh, and anecdotal evidence suggests that other social mammals may do too.  

对应段落：E段  

审题：题目问的是“除了（已知）科学家已经意识到哪些动物会笑，现在看来笑声可能比我们以前认为的更普遍。”需要填入一个动物名称。  

定位关键信息： 使用 "scientists have been aware" 和 "laugh" 定位到文中描述哪些动物被确认会笑的句子。 

解题思路：题目指出科学家已经知道某种动物会笑。在段落E中，原文明确提到“关系更远的灵长类动物，包括大猩猩，也会笑”。这表明“灵长类动物”是科学家已经确认会笑的动物类别。同时，由灵长类动物会笑引出对其他社会性哺乳动物也可能发笑的推测，从而证明笑可能更普遍。这与题目的逻辑完全一致。因此，答案为“primates”。


10

Although the reasons why humans started to laugh are still unknown,it seems that laughter may result from the 10  we feel with another person.

正确答案： E

原文答案句：By engaging in a bout of tickling, we put ourselves at the mercy of another individual, and laughing is a signal that we laughter is what makes it a reliable signal of trust according to Tom Flamson, a laughter researcher at the University of California, Los Angels. 

对应段落：F段

审题：题目问的是尽管人类开始笑的原因仍然未知，但似乎笑声可能源于我们与另一个人感受到的某种情感。

定位关键信息：通过题目中的"laugh"和"we feel with another person"可以定位到F段中关于笑声作为信任信号的描述。

解题思路：原文中提到"they get bonded to us as a result, which certainly seems like a show of trust"，表明笑声与信任有关。题目中的"we feel with another person"对应原文中的"bonded to us"，因此可以确定答案为E。


Questions 11-13


Do the following statements agree with the information given in the passage?


In boxes 11-13 on your answer sheet, write TRUE if the statement is true, FALSE if the statement is false, NOT GIVEN if the information is not given in the passage.


11

Both men and women laugh more when they are with members of the same sex.

正确答案： B. FALSE

原文答案句：Men tend to laugh longer and harder when they are with other men, perhaps as a way of bonding. Women tend to laugh more and at a higher pitch when men are present, possibly indicating flirtation or even submission.

对应段落：B段

审题：题目问“和同性在一起时，男性和女性都会笑得更多”，需要判断这一陈述是否与文章信息一致。

定位关键信息：利用题目中的“men and women”、“laugh more”和“with members of the same sex”定位到B段，该段讨论了男性和女性在不同社交场合的笑声表现。

解题思路：原文提到男性与男性在一起时会笑得更久、更响亮，而女性则是当男性在场时笑得更多，因此，题目中的男女都是同性在场笑得更多的说法，与原文信息不一致，因此，答案为FALSE。


12

Primates lack sufficient breath control to be able to produce laughs the way humans do.

正确答案： A. TRUE

原文答案句：While other animals pant, we alone can control our breath well enough to produce the sound of laughter. 

对应段落：G段

审题：题目问的是“灵长类动物缺乏足够的呼吸控制能力，无法像人类一样发出笑声”，需要判断这一陈述是否与文章信息一致。

定位关键信息：通过题目中的关键词"Primates"（灵长类动物）、"lack sufficient breath control"（缺乏足够的呼吸控制）和"produce laughs the way humans do"（像人类一样发出笑声）来定位文章中的相关句子。

解题思路：在G段中，原文明确指出“我们人类是唯一能够很好地控制呼吸以产生笑声的动物”，这意味着其他动物（包括灵长类动物）缺乏这种呼吸控制能力。因此，题目中的陈述与文章信息一致，答案为TRUE。


13

Chimpanzees produce laughter in a wider range of situations than rats do.

正确答案： C. NOT GIVEN

原文答案句：Other creatures, including chimpanzees, gorillas and even rats, chuckle. Obviously, they don’t crack up at Homer Simpson or titter at the boss’s dreadful jokes, but the fact that they laugh in the first place suggests that sniggers and chortles have been around for a lot longer than we have. 

对应段落：A段

审题：题目问的是“黑猩猩会发笑的情景，比老鼠要多得多。”是否与文章信息一致。

定位关键信息：利用题目中的“Chimpanzees”和“rats”定位到A段，文中提到黑猩猩、大猩猩和老鼠都会笑。

解题思路：原文提到黑猩猩、大猩猩和老鼠都会笑，但并没有比较它们发出笑声的情境范围。文中也没有提到黑猩猩比老鼠在更广泛的情境下发出笑声的信息。因此，题目中的比较信息在原文中没有给出，答案为NOT GIVEN。
